summary.brief: State v. Grad. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: On July 16, 2008, Grad was charged with three counts of endangering children under R.C. 2919.22(B)(1) and (E)(1)(d); three counts of felonious assault under R.C. 2903.11(A)(1); and two counts of endangering children under R.C. 2919.22(A)(1) and (E)(1)(c).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: When W.G. was 41 days old, his parents, Kenneth and Laura Grad, took him to a podiatrist because one of his feet appeared swollen.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: At Akron Children’s Hospital, an x-ray revealed that the tibia in W.G.’s left leg was fractured. It also revealed that W.G. had had other fractures that were in various stages of healing.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The state established the element of causation through a medical expert who used a process-of-elimination methodology: he opined that the appellant, Kenneth Grad, must have intentionally caused his son’s injuries because the expert could identify no nontraumatic medical cause of the bone fractures.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Steiner testified that on June 18, 2008, he examined W.G. and discussed this examination with the Grads.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Steiner

evidence_index.text: Dr. Steiner concluded that these events were not sufficient to cause the 26 bone fractures seen in W.G.’s x-rays.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Steiner

evidence_index.text: Dr. Steiner testified unequivocally that it could not.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Steiner

evidence_index.text: We conclude that the appellate court erred by affirming the trial court’s denial of Grad’s motion for leave to file a motion for a new trial without holding a hearing on that motion.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

evidence_index.text: The defendant must show that a posttrial change in scientific knowledge would provide him with a significantly stronger argument for his defense such that it could lead to a different outcome if trial were held today.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: Dr. Steiner then reiterated his ultimate opinion on the cause of W.G.’s bone fractures: that W.G. “suffered these fractures as a result of nonaccidental trauma. He was physically abused on multiple occasions over a period of several weeks.”
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Steiner

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Parents noticed swelling in one foot and sought medical attention.

focused_exam.musculoskeletal: Swelling in one foot, multiple fractures noted on imaging

critical_findings: Multiple fractures in various stages of healing

requestables.name: X-ray
requestables.description: X-ray of the affected limb and full skeletal survey

reveal.result_summary: Fracture of the tibia in the left leg, multiple fractures in various stages of healing
reveal.decision_impact: Prompted further investigation into potential abuse

reveal.clinical_significance: Ruled out a common genetic cause of bone fragility
reveal.decision_impact: Supported the hypothesis of non-accidental trauma

timeline_phases.name: Diagnostic Workup
timeline_phases.description: Imaging and initial genetic testing conducted

decision_points.clinical_context: Evaluation of multiple fractures in an infant
decision_points.prompt: What additional genetic testing should be considered?

options.description: Order comprehensive genetic testing including Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome
options.clinical_reasoning: Comprehensive testing could identify rare genetic conditions affecting bone fragility
Belief that initial tests were sufficient

actual_action_defendant.description: No further genetic testing was conducted
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Belief that initial tests were sufficient

expected_action_court.description: Comprehensive genetic testing should have been conducted
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony suggested further testing was warranted

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to conduct comprehensive genetic testing left potential genetic causes unexplored
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Comprehensive genetic testing could have identified a genetic condition

patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Potential for ongoing bone fragility issues

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to order genetic testing
Causation established - genetic disorder could explain fractures

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Genetic testing could have shown a bone fragility disorder

allegations.allegation: Failure to conduct comprehensive genetic testing
